lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret.proof_tex:
Put \(s=s_1\), \(q=\lceil s\rceil-1\), and let \(\phi(u)=(u^k:|k|\le q)\) be the finite vector of tensor monomials on \([0,1]^d\).  Let
\[
 k_m=\left\lceil m^{1/(2s+d)}\right\rceil,
 \qquad h_m=k_m^{-1},
\]
and partition \(\mathcal X\) into the \(k_m^d\) cubes of side \(h_m\).  If \(Q\) has lower corner \(c_Q\), write \(\phi_Q(x)=\phi((x-c_Q)/h_m)\) on \(Q\).  For \(a\in\{0,1\}\), define
\[
 G_{a,Q}=\frac2m\sum_{i=1}^m1\{A_i=a,X_i\in Q\}\phi_Q(X_i)\phi_Q(X_i)^\top,
\]
\[
 H_{a,Q}=\frac2m\sum_{i=1}^m1\{A_i=a,X_i\in Q\}Y_i\phi_Q(X_i).
\]
Let \(M_\phi=\int_{[0,1]^d}\phi(u)\phi(u)^\top du\) and \(\lambda_\star=\lambda_{\min}(M_\phi)/4>0\).  If \(\lambda_{\min}(G_{a,Q})\ge\lambda_\star h_m^d\), set \(\widehat b_{a,Q}=G_{a,Q}^{-1}H_{a,Q}\); otherwise set \(\widehat b_{a,Q}=0\).  For \(x\in Q\), define \(\widehat m_a(x)\) by truncating \(\phi_Q(x)^\top\widehat b_{a,Q}\) to \([-\bar B,\bar B]\).  This is a completely sample-measurable estimator.

We first establish its pointwise tail bound uniformly over \(P\in\mathcal P_1\).  Randomization gives
\[
 E_PG_{a,Q}=\int_Qg_P(x)\phi_Q(x)\phi_Q(x)^\top dx.
\]
The density lower bound implies
\[
 \lambda_{\min}(E_PG_{a,Q})
 \ge\frac12h_m^d\lambda_{\min}(M_\phi)
 =2\lambda_\star h_m^d.
\]
For every \(m_{P,a}\in C^s(\mathcal X)\), Taylor's formula on a cube gives a polynomial \(p_{a,Q}\) of degree at most \(q\) satisfying
\[
 \sup_{x\in Q}|m_{P,a}(x)-p_{a,Q}(x)|\le K_1h_m^s.
\]
For integer \(s\), this is the degree-\((s-1)\) Taylor bound using the bounded order-\(s\) derivatives; for noninteger \(s\), it is the degree-\(\lfloor s\rfloor\) Taylor bound using the stated Hölder modulus.  The constants are uniform because the Hölder radius is \(L_1\).

Let \(b^*_{a,Q}\) be the population weighted least-squares coefficient.  The preceding approximation, the density bounds, and the population Gram lower bound imply
\[
 \sup_{x\in Q}|m_{P,a}(x)-\phi_Q(x)^\top b^*_{a,Q}|\le K_2h_m^s,
 \qquad \|b^*_{a,Q}\|\le K_2.
\]
For independent bounded variables, scalar Bernstein's inequality reads
\[
 \Pr\{|m^{-1}\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^m(Z_i-EZ_i)|>t\}
 \le2\exp\!\left\{-\frac{mt^2}{2(\sigma^2+Mt/3)}\right\}
\]
when \(|Z_i-EZ_i|\le M\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(Z_i)\le\sigma^2\).  Apply this inequality to the finitely many entries of \(G_{a,Q}\) and to the coordinates of
\[
 \frac2m\sum_{i=1}^m1\{A_i=a,X_i\in Q\}
 \phi_Q(X_i)\{Y_i-\phi_Q(X_i)^\top b^*_{a,Q}\}.
\]
Each summand is bounded by a class-dependent constant and has variance at most a constant times \(h_m^d\).  A finite-dimensional union bound, the population eigenvalue bound, and the inverse perturbation identity therefore give constants \(K_3,k_3>0\) such that, for every fixed \(x\in Q\),
\[
 P^m\{|\widehat m_a(x)-m_{P,a}(x)|>K_3(h_m^s+u)\}
 \le K_3\exp(-k_3mh_m^du^2)+K_3\exp(-k_3mh_m^d)
\]
for \(0<u\le1\).  Truncation cannot increase the error because the true regression lies in \([-\bar B,\bar B]\).

Set
\[
 r_m=h_m^s+(mh_m^d)^{-1/2}\asymp m^{-s/(2s+d)}.
\]
The last display and a union bound over the two arms imply, uniformly in \(x\), a sub-Gaussian bound for \(|\widehat\Delta(x)-\Delta_P(x)|/r_m\) on its bounded range.  If \(\widehat d_m(x)\) chooses the wrong sign and \(\Delta_P(x)\ne0\), then
\[
 |\widehat\Delta(x)-\Delta_P(x)|\ge|\Delta_P(x)|.
\]
Split the nonzero contrast range into \(0<|\Delta_P|\le K_4r_m\) and the dyadic shells
\[
 2^jK_4r_m<|\Delta_P|\le2^{j+1}K_4r_m,
 \qquad j\ge0.
\]
The first region contributes at most \(K r_m^{\alpha+1}\) by the margin condition.  On shell \(j\), the pointwise tail bound and the margin condition give a contribution at most
\[
 K r_m^{\alpha+1}2^{j(\alpha+1)}e^{-k4^j}.
\]
The series over \(j\) is finite; the exponentially small Gram-failure term is smaller than every fixed power of \(r_m\).  Fubini's theorem consequently yields
\[
 \sup_{P\in\mathcal P_1}E_{P^m}
 E_{P_X}\!\left[|\Delta_P(X)|1\{\widehat d_m(X)\ne1\{\Delta_P(X)\ge0\}\}\mid\widehat d_m\right]
 \le K r_m^{\alpha+1}.
\]
The inner expectation is exactly the policy-value regret, with zero contribution from the tie set.  Since \(r_m^{\alpha+1}\asymp m^{-s(\alpha+1)/(2s+d)}\), the claimed near-tie bound follows.  On \(\mathcal P_0\), \(m_{P,0}=m_{P,1}\) almost surely, so every rule has the same value and the regret is identically zero.

snapshot.stmt:
There is an explicit cellwise local-polynomial estimator, based on \(m\) iid training observations and smoothness \(s_1\), whose induced rule \(\widehat d_m(x)=1\{\widehat m_1(x)\ge\widehat m_0(x)\}\) satisfies
\[
 \sup_{P\in\mathcal P_1}E_{P^m}\!\left[V(P)-E_P\{m_{P,\widehat d_m(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d_m\}\right]
 \le K m^{-s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)}.
\]
The constant \(K\) depends only on the frozen class parameters.  For every \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), the regret inside the expectation is identically zero for every training realization.

lem:parametric-tie-length-lower-bound.proof_tex:
Choose a fixed \(t>0\), to be made small below, and put \(\mu_n=t/\sqrt n\).  Define a full-data law \(F_{t,n}\) exactly as in the base construction except that, conditionally independently for \(a\in\{0,1\}\), the endpoint-supported potential outcome has mean \(E[Y(a)\mid X]=\mu_n\).  Thus
\[
 F_{t,n}\{Y(a)=B\mid X\}=\frac{1+\mu_n/B}{2},
 \qquad
 F_{t,n}\{Y(a)=-B\mid X\}=\frac{1-\mu_n/B}{2}.
\]
For all sufficiently large \(n\), these laws are dominated by \(\nu\), have uniform design density and propensity one half, satisfy consistency and exchangeability, have constant regressions \(m_0=m_1=\mu_n\), and obey
\[
 \operatorname{Var}(Y(a)\mid X)=B^2-\mu_n^2\ge v_0.
\]
Their constant regression norms are eventually below both \(L_0\) and \(L_1\), and the margin event \(\{0<|\Delta|\le u\}\) is empty.  Hence their observed images \(P_{t,n}\) belong to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\), while
\[
 V(P_{t,n})-V(P_0^\circ)=\mu_n.
\]

Under \(P_0^\circ\), the one-observation likelihood ratio is
\[
 \frac{dP_{t,n}}{dP_0^\circ}(O)=1+\frac{\mu_nY}{B^2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
 1+\chi^2(P_{t,n}^n,(P_0^\circ)^n)
 =\left(1+\frac{\mu_n^2}{B^2}\right)^n
 \le\exp(t^2/B^2).
\]
Choose \(t>0\) so small that
\[
 \rho_t:=\frac12\sqrt{e^{t^2/B^2}-1}<1-2\gamma.
\]
Then the total variation distance between the two product laws is at most \(\rho_t\).

For any union-honest interval sequence, uniform honesty applied to the base and the triangular sequence \(P_{t,n}\) gives coverage at least \(1-\gamma-r_n\) for some \(r_n\downarrow0\).  Transferring the alternative coverage event to the base law and intersecting it with base coverage yields
\[
 (P_0^\circ)^n\{0\in C_n,\ \mu_n\in C_n\}
 \ge1-2\gamma-2r_n-\rho_t.
\]
Connectedness forces length at least \(\mu_n\) on this event.  Consequently
\[
 \liminf_n n^{1/2}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]
 \ge t(1-2\gamma-\rho_t)=:c_\gamma>0.
\]
The final stratum-wise assertion follows from \(\mathrm{lem:nonempty-strata}\), which places \(P_0^\circ\) in both observed strata.

snapshot.stmt:
There is a constant \(c_\gamma>0\) such that every \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) satisfies
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}n^{1/2}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]\ge c_\gamma.
\]
The base law \(P_0^\circ\) belongs to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\), so this lower bound applies to both stratum-wise frontier criteria.

defs.def:admissible-pair: Write \(s=r+\tau\) with \(r=\lfloor s\rfloor\) and \(\tau\in[0,1)\); \(C^s(\mathcal X)\) has bounded continuous derivatives through order \(r\), with order-\(r\) derivatives \(\tau\)-Hölder when \(\tau>0\). Fix \(0<s_1<s_0\), \(L_0,L_1>0\), and \(\alpha,C>0\) satisfying \(s_1\alpha<1\), and choose \(\beta\in(s_1,1/\alpha)\).
defs.def:base-law: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, and conditionally independently draw \(Y(0),Y(1)\in\{-B,B\}\) with conditional means zero.  Let \(P_0^\circ\) be its observed image.
defs.def:sequence-frontier: For every positive deterministic \(a=(a_n)_n\), use \(\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) and \(\mathfrak R^{\mathrm{asy}}_j(a)\) exactly as defined in the symbol table; honesty is a property of the entire interval sequence.

thm:split-sample-union-upper-bracket.proof_tex:
Let \(m_n=\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) and \(q_n=n-m_n\). On the first \(m_n\) observations, construct the explicit local-polynomial rule \(\widehat d_{m_n}\) from \(\mathrm{lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret}\). On the remaining observations define
\[
 \widehat W_n
 =\frac1{q_n}\sum_{i=m_n+1}^n
 2\,1\{A_i=\widehat d_{m_n}(X_i)\}Y_i.
\]
Let \(K_{\mathrm{reg}}\) be the uniform constant in that lemma, set
\[
 \rho=\frac{s_1(\alpha+1)}{2s_1+d},
 \qquad
 b_n=\frac{2K_{\mathrm{reg}}}{\gamma}m_n^{-\rho},
 \qquad
 t_n=B\sqrt{\frac{8}{\gamma q_n}},
\]
and define the connected interval
\[
 C_n^\dagger=[\widehat W_n-t_n,\ \widehat W_n+t_n+b_n].
\]
This is a sample-measurable, almost surely finite closed interval.

Condition on the training sample, and abbreviate \(\widehat d=\widehat d_{m_n}\). Randomization gives
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 E_P[2\,1\{A=\widehat d(X)\}Y\mid X,\widehat d]
 &=m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X),\\
 W_P(\widehat d)
 &:=E_P[m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d].
 \end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(E_P[\widehat W_n\mid\widehat d]=W_P(\widehat d)\). Each validation summand lies in \([-2B,2B]\), so its conditional variance is at most \(4B^2\). Conditional independence of the validation observations and Markov's inequality applied to the squared centered mean yield
\[
 P^n\{|\widehat W_n-W_P(\widehat d)|>t_n\mid\widehat d\}
 \le\frac{4B^2}{q_nt_n^2}=\frac\gamma2.
\]

The policy regret
\[
 R_P(\widehat d)=V(P)-W_P(\widehat d)
 =E_P\!\left[\max_{a\in\{0,1\}}m_{P,a}(X)-m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d\right]
\]
is nonnegative. If \(P\in\mathcal P_1\), \(\mathrm{lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret}\) and Markov's inequality give
\[
 P^{m_n}\{R_P(\widehat d)>b_n\}
 \le\frac{K_{\mathrm{reg}}m_n^{-\rho}}{b_n}
 =\frac\gamma2.
\]
If \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), the same lemma states that \(R_P(\widehat d)=0\) for every training realization. On the intersection of the validation event \(|\widehat W_n-W_P(\widehat d)|\le t_n\) and the training event \(R_P(\widehat d)\le b_n\),
\[
 \widehat W_n-t_n\le W_P(\widehat d)\le V(P)
 \le W_P(\widehat d)+b_n\le\widehat W_n+t_n+b_n.
\]
A union bound therefore shows, for every \(n\ge2\),
\[
 \inf_{P\in\mathcal U}P^n\{V(P)\in C_n^\dagger\}\ge1-\gamma.
\]
Hence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\).

The interval length is deterministic and equals
\[
 \operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)=2t_n+b_n.
\]
Since \(m_n\) and \(q_n\) are each bounded below by a positive multiple of \(n\) for \(n\ge2\), there is a finite \(K_\dagger\), depending only on \(B,\gamma,K_{\mathrm{reg}},s_1,\alpha,d\), such that
\[
 2t_n+b_n\le K_\dagger\{n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\}.
\]
This bound is uniform over both strata. Inserting this single data-driven interval sequence into the infimum defining \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\) proves the final assertion. Notice that this construction uses no dependency on the multiscale handle in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\).

snapshot.stmt:
Let \(\rho=s_1(\alpha+1)/(2s_1+d)\). There is an explicit split-sample connected interval sequence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) such that
\[
 \sup_{P\in\mathcal P_j}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]
 \le K_\dagger\{n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\},
 \qquad j\in\{0,1\},
\]
for a finite constant \(K_\dagger\) depending only on the frozen class parameters and \(\gamma\). Consequently, for \(u_n=n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\), \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\le K_\dagger<\infty\) for both \(j=0\) and \(j=1\).

Fix \(0<s_1<s_0\), \(L_0,L_1>0\), and \(\alpha,C>0\) satisfying \(s_1\alpha<1\), and choose \(\beta\in(s_1,1/\alpha)\).

defs.def:union-upper-route: Construct a connected interval by fitting multiscale contrast estimates on training folds, using independent holdout folds to form simultaneous confidence bounds for their dyadic block coefficients, and calibrating the final interval to the maximum smoothing and nuisance bias over both \(\mathcal F_0\) and \(\mathcal F_1\).

for a finite constant \(K_\dagger\) depending only on the frozen class parameters and \(\gamma\). Consequently, for \(u_n=n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\), \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\le K_\dagger<\infty\) for both \(j=0\) and \(j=1\).
node.proof_tex:
Let \(m_n=\lfloor n/2\rfloor\) and \(q_n=n-m_n\). On the first \(m_n\) observations, construct the explicit local-polynomial rule \(\widehat d_{m_n}\) from \(\mathrm{lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret}\). On the remaining observations define
\[
 \widehat W_n
 =\frac1{q_n}\sum_{i=m_n+1}^n
 2\,1\{A_i=\widehat d_{m_n}(X_i)\}Y_i.
\]
Let \(K_{\mathrm{reg}}\) be the uniform constant in that lemma, set
\[
 \rho=\frac{s_1(\alpha+1)}{2s_1+d},
 \qquad
 b_n=\frac{2K_{\mathrm{reg}}}{\gamma}m_n^{-\rho},
 \qquad
 t_n=B\sqrt{\frac{8}{\gamma q_n}},
\]
and define the connected interval
\[
 C_n^\dagger=[\widehat W_n-t_n,\ \widehat W_n+t_n+b_n].
\]
This is a sample-measurable, almost surely finite closed interval.

Condition on the training sample, and abbreviate \(\widehat d=\widehat d_{m_n}\). Randomization gives
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 E_P[2\,1\{A=\widehat d(X)\}Y\mid X,\widehat d]
 &=m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X),\\
 W_P(\widehat d)
 &:=E_P[m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d].
 \end{aligned}
\]
Thus \(E_P[\widehat W_n\mid\widehat d]=W_P(\widehat d)\). Each validation summand lies in \([-2B,2B]\), so its conditional variance is at most \(4B^2\). Conditional independence of the validation observations and Markov's inequality applied to the squared centered mean yield
\[
 P^n\{|\widehat W_n-W_P(\widehat d)|>t_n\mid\widehat d\}
 \le\frac{4B^2}{q_nt_n^2}=\frac\gamma2.
\]

The policy regret
\[
 R_P(\widehat d)=V(P)-W_P(\widehat d)
 =E_P\!\left[\max_{a\in\{0,1\}}m_{P,a}(X)-m_{P,\widehat d(X)}(X)\mid\widehat d\right]
\]
is nonnegative. If \(P\in\mathcal P_1\), \(\mathrm{lem:local-polynomial-policy-regret}\) and Markov's inequality give
\[
 P^{m_n}\{R_P(\widehat d)>b_n\}
 \le\frac{K_{\mathrm{reg}}m_n^{-\rho}}{b_n}
 =\frac\gamma2.
\]
If \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), the same lemma states that \(R_P(\widehat d)=0\) for every training realization. On the intersection of the validation event \(|\widehat W_n-W_P(\widehat d)|\le t_n\) and the training event \(R_P(\widehat d)\le b_n\),
\[
 \widehat W_n-t_n\le W_P(\widehat d)\le V(P)
 \le W_P(\widehat d)+b_n\le\widehat W_n+t_n+b_n.
\]
A union bound therefore shows, for every \(n\ge2\),
\[
 \inf_{P\in\mathcal U}P^n\{V(P)\in C_n^\dagger\}\ge1-\gamma.
\]
Hence \((C_n^\dagger)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\).

The interval length is deterministic and equals
\[
 \operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)=2t_n+b_n.
\]
Since \(m_n\) and \(q_n\) are each bounded below by a positive multiple of \(n\) for \(n\ge2\), there is a finite \(K_\dagger\), depending only on \(B,\gamma,K_{\mathrm{reg}},s_1,\alpha,d\), such that
\[
 2t_n+b_n\le K_\dagger\{n^{-1/2}+n^{-\rho}\}.
\]
This bound is uniform over both strata. Inserting this single data-driven interval sequence into the infimum defining \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\) proves the final assertion. Notice that this construction uses no dependency on the multiscale handle in \(\mathrm{def:union-upper-route}\).
node.justification: The theorem supplies one explicit connected interval, constructed from a learned policy and an independent randomized evaluation sample, with finite-sample union coverage.
node.gap: Its regret allowance yields \(n^{-\rho}\), which is generally larger than the sparse lower scale \(n^{-\xi}\).
node.consumer: It gives an immediately implementable conservative route for the rilpivirine-versus-efavirenz optimal-value analysis.

lem:mean-envelope.proof_tex:
Fix \(a\in\{0,1\}\).  By consistency, on \(\{A=a\}\) one has \(Y=Y(a)\).  Conditional exchangeability and the positivity \(P(A=a\mid X)=1/2\) supplied by known randomization therefore give, for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\),
\[
 m_{P,a}(x)=E_P[Y\mid A=a,X=x]
 =E_F[Y(a)\mid A=a,X=x]
 =E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x].
\]
This proves the asserted identification without defining the causal quantity to be the observed regression.

By bounded outcomes, \(E_F[Y(a)^2\mid X=x]\le B^2\).  The conditional variance identity and \(\mathrm{ass:variance-floor}\) now imply
\[
 v_0\le \operatorname{Var}_F(Y(a)\mid X=x)
 =E_F[Y(a)^2\mid X=x]-E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x]^2
 \le B^2-m_{P,a}(x)^2.
\]
Consequently \(m_{P,a}(x)^2\le B^2-v_0=\bar B^2\), and hence \(|m_{P,a}(x)|\le\bar B\), for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\).

snapshot.stmt: Under \(\mathrm{ass:consistency}\), \(\mathrm{ass:exchangeability}\), \(\mathrm{ass:randomization}\), \(\mathrm{ass:bounded-outcomes}\), and \(\mathrm{ass:variance-floor}\), consistency, conditional exchangeability, and positivity from known randomization identify \(m_{P,a}(x)=E_F[Y(a)\mid X=x]\) for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\) and each \(a\in\{0,1\}\); consequently \(|m_{P,a}(x)|\le\bar B=\sqrt{B^2-v_0}\) for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\).

lem:nonempty-strata.proof_tex:
Let \(F^\circ\) denote the full-data law specified in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\). Because \(X\) is uniform on \(\mathcal X=[0,1]^d\), \(A\) has mass \(1/2\) at each point of \(\{0,1\}\), and each potential outcome is supported on the two atoms \(\{-B,B\}\), the product construction gives \(F^\circ\ll\nu\). Indeed, relative to the factors of \(\nu=\lambda_d\otimes\#_{\{0,1\}}\otimes\kappa_B\otimes\kappa_B\), the corresponding factor densities are respectively \(1\), \(1/2\), and, for each potential outcome, \(1/2\) on each of the atoms \(-B\) and \(B\) and zero elsewhere.

The observation map gives \(Y=Y(A)\), so consistency holds. The conditional mean-zero requirement and the support \(\{-B,B\}\) force
\[
F^\circ\{Y(a)=B\mid X,A\}=F^\circ\{Y(a)=-B\mid X,A\}=\frac12,
\qquad a\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Thus the conditional law of \((Y(0),Y(1))\) given \((X,A)\) does not depend on \(A\), which proves \((Y(0),Y(1))\perp A\mid X\). Independence of \(A\) and \(X\) gives \(e_{P_0^\circ}(x)=1/2\), while uniformity of \(X\) gives \(g_{P_0^\circ}(x)=1\), in each case for \(\lambda_d\)-almost every \(x\). Moreover, \(|Y(a)|=B\) almost surely and
\[
\operatorname{Var}_{F^\circ}(Y(a)\mid X)=B^2\ge v_0,
\]
where the last inequality follows from \(v_0\in(0,B^2)\).

For each \(a\in\{0,1\}\), consistency and the preceding conditional distribution yield
\[
m_{P_0^\circ,a}(x)=E_{P_0^\circ}[Y\mid A=a,X=x]=0
\]
for \(P_X\)-almost every \(x\). Hence \(\lVert m_{P_0^\circ,a}\rVert_{C^{s_0}(\mathcal X)}=0\le L_0\), and \(\Delta_{P_0^\circ}=0\) almost everywhere. All defining conditions in \(\mathrm{def:exact-tie-stratum}\) therefore hold, so \(F^\circ\in\mathcal F_0\).

Now fix any admissible pair from \(\mathrm{def:admissible-pair}\) and regard the same \(F^\circ\) as the zero-contrast law. The domination, consistency, exchangeability, randomization, boundedness, variance, and density calculations above are unchanged. Since the zero function belongs to every Hölder class with norm zero,
\[
\max_{a\in\{0,1\}}\lVert m_{P_0^\circ,a}\rVert_{C^{s_1}(\mathcal X)}=0\le L_1.
\]
For every \(u\in(0,2\bar B]\), the event \(\{0<|\Delta_{P_0^\circ}(X)|\le u\}\) is empty, and consequently
\[
P_X\{0<|\Delta_{P_0^\circ}(X)|\le u\}=0\le Cu^\alpha.
\]
Thus every defining condition in \(\mathrm{def:near-tie-stratum}\) holds and \(F^\circ\in\mathcal F_1\).

Finally, by \(\mathrm{def:observed-strata}\), the observed image \(P_0^\circ=T_\#F^\circ\) belongs to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\). Hence both observed-data strata are nonempty, and therefore their union \(\mathcal U=\mathcal P_0\cup\mathcal P_1\) is nonempty.

snapshot.stmt: The base law in \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\) belongs to \(\mathcal F_0\).  For every admissible pair, the zero-contrast law also belongs to \(\mathcal F_1\); hence both observed-data strata and \(\mathcal U\) are nonempty.

defs.def:admissible-pair: Write \(s=r+\tau\) with \(r=\lfloor s\rfloor\) and \(\tau\in[0,1)\); \(C^s(\mathcal X)\) has bounded continuous derivatives through order \(r\), with order-\(r\) derivatives \(\tau\)-Hölder when \(\tau>0\). Fix \(0<s_1<s_0\), \(L_0,L_1>0\), and \(\alpha,C>0\) satisfying \(s_1\alpha<1\), and choose \(\beta\in(s_1,1/\alpha)\).
defs.def:base-law: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, and conditionally independently draw \(Y(0),Y(1)\in\{-B,B\}\) with conditional means zero.  Let \(P_0^\circ\) be its observed image.

prop:oracle-tie-rootn.proof_tex:
Fix \(P\in\mathcal P_0\).  The exact-tie condition gives \(m_{P,1}(X)=m_{P,0}(X)\) almost surely, and thus
\[
 V(P)=E_P[\max\{m_{P,0}(X),m_{P,1}(X)\}]=E_P[m_{P,0}(X)].
\]
Randomization and iterated expectation yield
\[
 E_P[2\,1\{A=0\}Y\mid X]
 =2P(A=0\mid X)E_P[Y\mid A=0,X]
 =m_{P,0}(X).
\]
Averaging over \(X\) proves \(E_P[\widehat V_{0,n}]=V(P)\).

Set \(Z_i=2\,1\{A_i=0\}Y_i\).  Under iid sampling, \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) are independent, have common mean \(V(P)\), and lie in \([-2B,2B]\).  Hoeffding's inequality, in its bounded-range form,
\[
 P^n\!\left\{\left|n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i-E_PZ_i\right|\ge t\right\}
 \le 2\exp\!\left\{-\frac{2n t^2}{(4B)^2}\right\}
 =2\exp\!\left\{-\frac{nt^2}{8B^2}\right\},
\]
with \(t=B\sqrt{8\log(2/\gamma)/n}\), gives
\[
 P^n\{V(P)\in C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}}\}\ge1-\gamma.
\]
Finally, the interval length is nonrandom and equals
\[
 2B\sqrt{8\log(2/\gamma)/n}.
\]
Taking the supremum over \(P\in\mathcal P_0\) proves the claimed \(O(n^{-1/2})\) bound.

snapshot.stmt: For every \(P\in\mathcal P_0\), \(E_P[\widehat V_{0,n}]=V(P)\), and \(P^n\{V(P)\in C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}}\}\ge1-\gamma\).  Consequently \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_0}E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_{0,n}^{\mathrm{or}})]=O(n^{-1/2})\).

lem:sparse-family-membership.proof_tex:
Write \(s=s_1\), let
\[
 J_1=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)\,dz>0,
 \qquad
 J_2=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)^2\,dz>0,
\]
and extend \(\psi\) by zero as in the symbol declaration.  Since \(\beta>s\), every derivative required by the definition of \(C^s\) vanishes continuously at the boundary of the cube, and the order-\(\lfloor s\rfloor\) derivatives have the required Hölder modulus.  Consequently there is a finite constant \(M_\psi\), depending only on \((d,s,\beta)\), such that for every \(h\in(0,1)\), every collection of disjoint cubes \(Q_\ell=c_\ell+h[0,1]^d\), and every signs \(\vartheta_\ell\in\{-1,1\}\),
\[
 \left\|h^s\sum_\ell \vartheta_\ell
 \psi\!\left(\frac{\cdot-c_\ell}{h}\right)\right\|_{C^s(\mathcal X)}
 \le M_\psi.
\]
Indeed, scaling gives this bound within one cube.  Between two cubes, the boundary vanishing of all derivatives through order \(\lfloor s\rfloor\) bounds the cross-cube Hölder quotient by the same constant.  Disjointness ensures that no point receives two nonzero summands.

We next verify the margin inequality rather than treating it as part of the construction.  For \(z\in[0,1]\), put \(r(z)=2\min\{z,1-z\}\).  Then \(4z(1-z)\ge r(z)\), and \(r(Z)\) is uniform on \([0,1]\) when \(Z\) is uniform.  Iterated integration gives, for independent uniform \(R_1,\ldots,R_d\) and \(q\in(0,1]\),
\[
 \Pr\!\left\{\prod_{j=1}^dR_j\le q\right\}
 =q\sum_{k=0}^{d-1}\frac{\{\log(1/q)\}^k}{k!}.
\]
Because \(\alpha\beta<1\), the right-hand side at \(q=t^{1/\beta}\) is at most \(M_{\alpha,\beta,d}t^\alpha\), uniformly over \(t\in(0,1]\).  The same bound, after increasing the constant, is trivial for \(t\ge1\).  Hence
\[
 \lambda_d\{z\in(0,1)^d:0<\psi(z)\le t\}
 \le M_{\alpha,\beta,d}t^\alpha,
 \qquad t>0.
\]
Since the cubes are disjoint and \(X\) is uniform under the construction,
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 P_X\{0<|\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)|\le u\}
 &=N_nh_n^d\lambda_d\{0<\psi\le u/a_n\}\\
 &\le M_{\alpha,\beta,d}N_nh_n^d(u/a_n)^\alpha\\
 &\le M_{\alpha,\beta,d}c_Nc_a^{-\alpha}u^\alpha.
 \end{aligned}
\]
Thus choosing the fixed calibration so that \(M_{\alpha,\beta,d}c_Nc_a^{-\alpha}\le C\) proves \(\mathrm{ass:margin-near-tie}\).

Now consider the full-data law in \(\mathrm{def:sparse-near-tie-family}\).  Uniform \(X\), randomized \(A\), and endpoint-supported potential outcomes give domination by \(\nu\), consistency under the observation map, and conditional exchangeability because \(A\) is independent of \((X,Y(0),Y(1))\).  They also give \(g_P=1\), \(e_P=1/2\), and bounded outcomes.  The observed regressions are
\[
 m_{P_{\theta,n},1}(x)=\frac{\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)}2,
 \qquad
 m_{P_{\theta,n},0}(x)=-\frac{\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)}2.
\]
The scaling bound above gives
\[
 \max_{a\in\{0,1\}}\|m_{P_{\theta,n},a}\|_{C^s(\mathcal X)}
 \le \frac{M_\psi c_a}{2}.
\]
Choose \(c_a\le2L_1/M_\psi\).  Also \(\|\Delta_{\theta,n}\|_\infty\le a_n\to0\), so eventually \(|\Delta_{\theta,n}|/2\le\bar B\).  For the endpoint Rademacher outcome with mean \((2a-1)\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\),
\[
 \operatorname{Var}_F(Y(a)\mid X=x)
 =B^2-\frac{\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)^2}{4}
 \ge B^2-\bar B^2=v_0.
\]
All conditions defining \(\mathcal F_1\) now hold, so \(F_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal F_1\), and its observed image belongs to \(\mathcal P_1\).

The base law has both regressions equal to zero, hence \(V(P_0^\circ)=0\).  Under an alternative,
\[
 \max\{m_{P_{\theta,n},0}(x),m_{P_{\theta,n},1}(x)\}
 =\frac{|\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)|}{2}.
\]
The signs disappear after taking the absolute value, and a change of variables on every cube gives
\[
 V(P_{\theta,n})-V(P_0^\circ)
 =\frac12\int_{\mathcal X}|\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)|\,dx
 =\frac{a_nN_nh_n^dJ_1}{2}
 =\delta_n.
\]
Finally, \(s\alpha<1\le d\), so \(h_n^{s\alpha-d}\to\infty\) and
\[
 N_n\sim c_Nh_n^{s\alpha-d}.
\]
Therefore
\[
 \delta_n\asymp h_n^s h_n^{s\alpha-d}h_n^d
 =h_n^{s(\alpha+1)}
 \asymp n^{-2s(\alpha+1)/(d+s\alpha+4s)}
 =n^{-\xi}.
\]

snapshot.stmt: For all sufficiently large \(n\) and every \(\theta\in\{-1,1\}^{N_n}\), \(F_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal F_1\), \(P_{\theta,n}\in\mathcal P_1\), and \(V(P_{\theta,n})-V(P_0^\circ)=\delta_n\).  Moreover \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\).

defs.def:admissible-pair: Write \(s=r+\tau\) with \(r=\lfloor s\rfloor\) and \(\tau\in[0,1)\); \(C^s(\mathcal X)\) has bounded continuous derivatives through order \(r\), with order-\(r\) derivatives \(\tau\)-Hölder when \(\tau>0\). Fix \(0<s_1<s_0\), \(L_0,L_1>0\), and \(\alpha,C>0\) satisfying \(s_1\alpha<1\), and choose \(\beta\in(s_1,1/\alpha)\).
defs.def:base-law: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, and conditionally independently draw \(Y(0),Y(1)\in\{-B,B\}\) with conditional means zero.  Let \(P_0^\circ\) be its observed image.
defs.def:sparse-near-tie-family: For each sufficiently large \(n\), use \(\mathcal C_n\) and \(\Delta_{\theta,n}\). Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, set \(E_F[Y(1)\mid X=x]=\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\) and \(E_F[Y(0)\mid X=x]=-\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\), and conditionally independently draw \(Y(a)\in\{-B,B\}\) with the corresponding Rademacher probabilities.  The constants \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) are fixed small enough that every displayed mean lies in \([-\bar B,\bar B]\), every conditional variance is at least \(v_0\), and the Hölder and margin bounds hold.

thm:sparse-near-tie-adaptation-lower-bou.proof_tex:
Put \(R_n=(P_0^\circ)^n\), and let \(L_{\theta,n}=dP_{\theta,n}/dP_0^\circ\) denote the one-observation likelihood ratio. Under the base law, conditional on \((X,A)\), the observed outcome is uniform on \(\{-B,B\}\). Under \(P_{\theta,n}\), its conditional mean is \((2A-1)\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)/2\). Therefore
\[
 L_{\theta,n}(O)=1+\frac{(2A-1)Y\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)}{2B^2}.
\]
Let
\[
 J_2=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)^2\,dz
 \quad\text{and}\quad
 q_n=\frac{a_n^2h_n^dJ_2}{4B^2}.
\]
For two sign vectors \(\theta\) and \(\theta'\), independence and symmetry under \(P_0^\circ\), followed by disjointness of the cell supports, give
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 E_{P_0^\circ}[L_{\theta,n}L_{\theta',n}]
 &=1+\frac{1}{4B^2}\int_{\mathcal X}\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)\Delta_{\theta',n}(x)\,dx\\
 &=1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}\theta_\ell\theta'_\ell.
 \end{aligned}
\]
Taking products over the \(n\) independent observations and then averaging over the two sign vectors yields
\[
 1+\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 =2^{-2N_n}\sum_{\theta,\theta'}
 \left(1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}\theta_\ell\theta'_\ell\right)^n.
\]
If \(R_1,\ldots,R_{N_n}\) are independent Rademacher variables, the last display equals
\[
 E\left(1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}R_\ell\right)^n.
\]
The factor inside the power is nonnegative because it is an integral of a product of nonnegative likelihood ratios. The elementary inequalities \(1+x\le e^x\) for \(x\ge-1\) and \(\cosh(t)\le e^{t^2/2}\) therefore imply
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 1+\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 &\le E\exp\left(nq_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}R_\ell\right)\\
 &=\{\cosh(nq_n)\}^{N_n}\\
 &\le\exp\left(\frac{N_nn^2q_n^2}{2}\right)\\
 &=\exp\left\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\right\},
 \end{aligned}
\]
where one may take \(K=J_2^2/(32B^4)\).

We next check that the testing calibration works for every admissible pair, without imposing \(3s_1\alpha<d\). Since \(N_n\le c_Nh_n^{s_1\alpha-d}\), \(a_n=c_ah_n^{s_1}\), \(D=d+s_1\alpha+4s_1\), and \(h_n=c_hn^{-2/D}\),
\[
 n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}
 \le c_Nc_a^4n^2h_n^{d+s_1\alpha+4s_1}
 =c_Nc_a^4c_h^D.
\]
Fix any \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\). First choose \(c_a>0\) small enough for the Hölder and mean-envelope requirements in \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\); next choose \(c_N>0\) small enough for its margin requirement; finally choose \(c_h>0\) small enough that
\[
 Kc_Nc_a^4c_h^D\le\log(1+\varepsilon^2).
\]
These choices are fixed in \(n\) and satisfy all of the membership and testing restrictions simultaneously. Moreover, \(s_1\alpha<1\le d\), so \(N_nh_n^d\asymp h_n^{s_1\alpha}\to0\); hence the required \(N_n\) disjoint cells fit inside \([1/4,3/4]^d\) for all sufficiently large \(n\). We conclude that
\[
 \chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 \le \exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1
 \le\varepsilon^2
\]
eventually for every admissible pair.

By the declared type of the base law and \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\), \(P_0^\circ\in\mathcal P_0\subset\mathcal U\) and \(V(P_0^\circ)=0\). By \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\), every component of \(\overline Q_n\) belongs to \(\mathcal P_1\subset\mathcal U\) and has value \(\delta_n\). Fix an arbitrary \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\). The definition of uniform asymptotic honesty supplies a deterministic sequence \(r_n\downarrow0\) such that, eventually,
\[
 R_n\{0\in C_n\}\ge1-\gamma-r_n,
 \qquad
 \overline Q_n\{\delta_n\in C_n\}\ge1-\gamma-r_n.
\]
For every event \(E\), Cauchy--Schwarz applied to \(d\overline Q_n/dR_n-1\) gives
\[
 |\overline Q_n(E)-R_n(E)|
 \le\frac12\{\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)\}^{1/2}
 \le\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
 R_n\{0\in C_n,\ \delta_n\in C_n\}
 \ge1-2\gamma-2r_n-\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]
Because \(C_n\) is connected, this event implies \(\operatorname{length}(C_n)\ge\delta_n\). Thus
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}
 \frac{E_{R_n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]}{\delta_n}
 \ge1-2\gamma-\frac{\varepsilon}{2}
 =:c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0.
\]
This proves the chi-square and expected-length assertions for every admissible pair.

For the final conditional comparison, \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\) gives \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), and direct algebra gives
\[
 \xi-\frac12
 =\frac{3s_1\alpha-d}{2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)}.
\]
If \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\) and \(\delta_n/n^{-1/2}\to\infty\) up to fixed positive multiplicative constants. The just-proved union-honest lower bound is then strictly slower than root \(n\) at \(P_0^\circ\), while \(\mathrm{prop:oracle-tie-rootn}\) gives an \(O(n^{-1/2})\)-length interval when coverage is required only over the exact-tie stratum.

snapshot.stmt: For every admissible pair, there are fixed admissible choices of \(c_h,c_a,c_N\) and \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\) such that \(\chi^2(\overline Q_n,(P_0^\circ)^n)\le\exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1\le\varepsilon^2\) eventually. Hence every \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\) obeys \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]/\delta_n\ge c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0\). If, in addition, \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\), so union honesty forces a strictly slower-than-root-\(n\) expected length at an exact-tie law even though \(\mathcal P_0\) alone admits \(O(n^{-1/2})\) length.

defs.def:admissible-pair: Write \(s=r+\tau\) with \(r=\lfloor s\rfloor\) and \(\tau\in[0,1)\); \(C^s(\mathcal X)\) has bounded continuous derivatives through order \(r\), with order-\(r\) derivatives \(\tau\)-Hölder when \(\tau>0\). defs.def:base-law: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, and conditionally independently draw \(Y(0),Y(1)\in\{-B,B\}\) with conditional means zero.  Let \(P_0^\circ\) be its observed image.
defs.def:sparse-near-tie-family: For each sufficiently large \(n\), use \(\mathcal C_n\) and \(\Delta_{\theta,n}\). Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Unif}(\mathcal X)\), let \(A\sim\operatorname{Bernoulli}(1/2)\) independently, set \(E_F[Y(1)\mid X=x]=\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\) and \(E_F[Y(0)\mid X=x]=-\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)/2\), and conditionally independently draw \(Y(a)\in\{-B,B\}\) with the corresponding Rademacher probabilities. thm:honest-adaptation-phase-bracket.proof_tex:
Fix an arbitrary honest interval sequence \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\), and write
\[
 e_n=E_{(P_0^\circ)^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)].
\]
By \(\mathrm{lem:parametric-tie-length-lower-bound}\), there is \(c_\gamma>0\) such that
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}\frac{e_n}{n^{-1/2}}\ge c_\gamma.
\]
By \(\mathrm{thm:sparse-near-tie-adaptation-lower-bound}\), for the fixed admissible calibration and some \(c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0\),
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}\frac{e_n}{\delta_n}\ge c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}.
\]
Consequently, for all sufficiently large \(n\),
\[
 e_n\ge\frac{c_\gamma}{2}n^{-1/2},
 \qquad
 e_n\ge\frac{c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}}{2}\delta_n,
\]
and hence
\[
 e_n\ge c_*\{n^{-1/2}\vee\delta_n\},
 \qquad
 c_*:=\frac12\min\{c_\gamma,c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}\}>0.
\]
The base law belongs to both \(\mathcal P_0\) and \(\mathcal P_1\) by \(\mathrm{lem:parametric-tie-length-lower-bound}\). Therefore, for each \(j\in\{0,1\}\),
\[
 \limsup_{n\to\infty}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_j}
 \frac{E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]}{\ell_n}
 \ge c_*.
\]
Taking the infimum over all honest sequences proves \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(\ell)\ge c_*\).

The upper assertion follows from the single interval sequence constructed in \(\mathrm{thm:split-sample-union-upper-bracket}\): for a finite \(K_*\), it satisfies
\[
 \limsup_{n\to\infty}\sup_{P\in\mathcal P_j}
 \frac{E_{P^n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n^\dagger)]}{u_n}
 \le K_*,
 \qquad j\in\{0,1\}.
\]
Inserting it into each defining infimum gives \(\mathfrak R_j^{\mathrm{asy}}(u)\le K_*\).

By \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\), \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), so
\[
 \ell_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\xi\}},
 \qquad
 u_n\asymp n^{-\min\{1/2,\rho\}}.
\]
The admissibility condition \(s_1\alpha<1\le d\) implies \(d-s_1\alpha>0\), and direct subtraction gives
\[
 \xi-\rho
 =\frac{s_1(\alpha+1)(d-s_1\alpha)}{(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)(d+2s_1)}>0.
\]
Also,
\[
 \rho-\frac12=\frac{2s_1\alpha-d}{2(2s_1+d)},
 \qquad
 \xi-\frac12=\frac{3s_1\alpha-d}{2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)}.
\]
If \(2s_1\alpha\ge d\), then \(\rho\ge1/2\) and \(\xi>\rho\), so both scales are root \(n\). If \(2s_1\alpha<d\le3s_1\alpha\), then \(\rho<1/2\le\xi\), giving the second regime. If \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\rho<\xi<1/2\), giving the third regime.

\]
There are constants \(0<c_*\le K_*<\infty\), depending only on the frozen class parameters and \(\gamma\), such that for both \(j\in\{0,1\}\),

proofs.proof_tex:
Put \(R_n=(P_0^\circ)^n\), and let \(L_{\theta,n}=dP_{\theta,n}/dP_0^\circ\) denote the one-observation likelihood ratio. Under the base law, conditional on \((X,A)\), the observed outcome is uniform on \(\{-B,B\}\). Under \(P_{\theta,n}\), its conditional mean is \((2A-1)\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)/2\). Therefore
\[
 L_{\theta,n}(O)=1+\frac{(2A-1)Y\Delta_{\theta,n}(X)}{2B^2}.
\]
Let
\[
 J_2=\int_{[0,1]^d}\psi(z)^2\,dz
 \quad\text{and}\quad
 q_n=\frac{a_n^2h_n^dJ_2}{4B^2}.
\]
For two sign vectors \(\theta\) and \(\theta'\), independence and symmetry under \(P_0^\circ\), followed by disjointness of the cell supports, give
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 E_{P_0^\circ}[L_{\theta,n}L_{\theta',n}]
 &=1+\frac{1}{4B^2}\int_{\mathcal X}\Delta_{\theta,n}(x)\Delta_{\theta',n}(x)\,dx\\
 &=1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}\theta_\ell\theta'_\ell.
 \end{aligned}
\]
Taking products over the \(n\) independent observations and then averaging over the two sign vectors yields
\[
 1+\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 =2^{-2N_n}\sum_{\theta,\theta'}
 \left(1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}\theta_\ell\theta'_\ell\right)^n.
\]
If \(R_1,\ldots,R_{N_n}\) are independent Rademacher variables, the last display equals
\[
 E\left(1+q_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}R_\ell\right)^n.
\]
The factor inside the power is nonnegative because it is an integral of a product of nonnegative likelihood ratios. The elementary inequalities \(1+x\le e^x\) for \(x\ge-1\) and \(\cosh(t)\le e^{t^2/2}\) therefore imply
\[
 \begin{aligned}
 1+\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 &\le E\exp\left(nq_n\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_n}R_\ell\right)\\
 &=\{\cosh(nq_n)\}^{N_n}\\
 &\le\exp\left(\frac{N_nn^2q_n^2}{2}\right)\\
 &=\exp\left\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\right\},
 \end{aligned}
\]
where one may take \(K=J_2^2/(32B^4)\).

We next check that the testing calibration works for every admissible pair, without imposing \(3s_1\alpha<d\). Since \(N_n\le c_Nh_n^{s_1\alpha-d}\), \(a_n=c_ah_n^{s_1}\), \(D=d+s_1\alpha+4s_1\), and \(h_n=c_hn^{-2/D}\),
\[
 n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}
 \le c_Nc_a^4n^2h_n^{d+s_1\alpha+4s_1}
 =c_Nc_a^4c_h^D.
\]
Fix any \(\varepsilon\in(0,1-2\gamma)\). First choose \(c_a>0\) small enough for the Hölder and mean-envelope requirements in \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\); next choose \(c_N>0\) small enough for its margin requirement; finally choose \(c_h>0\) small enough that
\[
 Kc_Nc_a^4c_h^D\le\log(1+\varepsilon^2).
\]
These choices are fixed in \(n\) and satisfy all of the membership and testing restrictions simultaneously. Moreover, \(s_1\alpha<1\le d\), so \(N_nh_n^d\asymp h_n^{s_1\alpha}\to0\); hence the required \(N_n\) disjoint cells fit inside \([1/4,3/4]^d\) for all sufficiently large \(n\). We conclude that
\[
 \chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)
 \le \exp\{K n^2N_na_n^4h_n^{2d}\}-1
 \le\varepsilon^2
\]
eventually for every admissible pair.

By the declared type of the base law and \(\mathrm{def:base-law}\), \(P_0^\circ\in\mathcal P_0\subset\mathcal U\) and \(V(P_0^\circ)=0\). By \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\), every component of \(\overline Q_n\) belongs to \(\mathcal P_1\subset\mathcal U\) and has value \(\delta_n\). Fix an arbitrary \((C_n)_n\in\mathfrak C^{\mathrm{asy}}(\mathcal U,\gamma)\). The definition of uniform asymptotic honesty supplies a deterministic sequence \(r_n\downarrow0\) such that, eventually,
\[
 R_n\{0\in C_n\}\ge1-\gamma-r_n,
 \qquad
 \overline Q_n\{\delta_n\in C_n\}\ge1-\gamma-r_n.
\]
For every event \(E\), Cauchy--Schwarz applied to \(d\overline Q_n/dR_n-1\) gives
\[
 |\overline Q_n(E)-R_n(E)|
 \le\frac12\{\chi^2(\overline Q_n,R_n)\}^{1/2}
 \le\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
 R_n\{0\in C_n,\ \delta_n\in C_n\}
 \ge1-2\gamma-2r_n-\frac{\varepsilon}{2}.
\]
Because \(C_n\) is connected, this event implies \(\operatorname{length}(C_n)\ge\delta_n\). Thus
\[
 \liminf_{n\to\infty}
 \frac{E_{R_n}[\operatorname{length}(C_n)]}{\delta_n}
 \ge1-2\gamma-\frac{\varepsilon}{2}
 =:c_{\gamma,\varepsilon}>0.
\]
This proves the chi-square and expected-length assertions for every admissible pair.

For the final conditional comparison, \(\mathrm{lem:sparse-family-membership}\) gives \(\delta_n\asymp n^{-\xi}\), and direct algebra gives
\[
 \xi-\frac12
 =\frac{3s_1\alpha-d}{2(d+s_1\alpha+4s_1)}.
\]
If \(3s_1\alpha<d\), then \(\xi<1/2\) and \(\delta_n/n^{-1/2}\to\infty\) up to fixed positive multiplicative constants. The just-proved union-honest lower bound is then strictly slower than root \(n\) at \(P_0^\circ\), while \(\mathrm{prop:oracle-tie-rootn}\) gives an \(O(n^{-1/2})\)-length interval when coverage is required only over the exact-tie stratum.
